title: Lecture for Workshop on Random Matrices and Random Systems
speaker: Balint Virag

proof_stages.label: Dyson beta edge-universality target
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines Dyson beta ensembles, states edge universality of the top eigenvalues after centering at the equilibrium edge and N^{2/3} scaling, surveys existing proofs, and announces a probabilistic change-of-coordinates approach for uniformly convex polynomial potentials.

proof_stages.label: Jacobi spectral coordinates
proof_stages.summary: Tridiagonalization from the first coordinate produces a Jacobi matrix with the same eigenvalues and spectral weights, giving a nonlinear diffeomorphism between Jacobi coefficients and eigenvalue/weight data tied to orthogonal polynomials and inverse spectral theory.

proof_stages.label: Gaussian tridiagonal operator paradigm
proof_stages.summary: For the quadratic potential, the Jacobi coefficients become independent normal and chi variables, so the beta ensemble is represented as a discrete random Schrodinger operator whose edge and bulk limits can be analyzed as operator limits.

proof_stages.label: Jacobian transfer to Jacobi coefficients
proof_stages.summary: The identities B_2=Q\Delta and the Jacobian factor B_1 transform the beta-ensemble density, augmented by Dirichlet spectral weights, into a density on Jacobi coefficients proportional to exp[-n Tr V(T)] times a product of powers of the off-diagonal entries.

proof_stages.label: Finite-range Markov/Gibbs structure
proof_stages.summary: Expanding Tr V(T) as closed paths shows that the transformed Jacobi-coefficient law is independent in the Gaussian case and, for a general polynomial potential, a finite-memory Markov/Gibbs chain with a slowly varying external field and an initial boundary condition.

proof_stages.label: Norm-resolvent convergence to the stochastic Airy operator
proof_stages.summary: The rescaled operator N^{2/3}(E-T), embedded near the first N^{1/3} Jacobi coordinates, is asserted to converge in norm resolvent sense to the stochastic Airy operator -d^2/dt^2+t+(2/sqrt(beta))B' with Dirichlet boundary conditions, implying convergence of eigenvalues and eigenvectors.

proof_stages.label: RRV integrated-potential criterion
proof_stages.summary: A prior convergence theorem reduces the proof to checking tightness and especially that partial sums of centered Jacobi-coefficient combinations converge uniformly on compact time intervals to the deterministic quadratic drift plus Brownian motion appearing in the stochastic Airy potential.

proof_stages.label: Mixing, boundary cutoff, and Gaussian approximation
proof_stages.summary: The incorrect first-order behavior caused by the initial boundary condition is removed using exponential mixing and a logarithmic cutoff, eigenvalue effects of the cutoff are bounded by Rayleigh arguments, and convexity/Brascamp-Lieb estimates plus Gaussian approximation of mesoscopic chunks yield the Brownian limit required by the criterion.

examples.name: de Branges' inverse-spectral program for zeta zeros
examples.note: The speaker cites de Branges' attempt to realize zeros of the Riemann zeta function as spectral data as an optional illustration of the breadth of the Jacobi/spectral-measure correspondence.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker cites de Branges trying to build an operator whose eigenvalues are zeta zeros, but does not carry out any construction or calculation for it

passes.reason: The de Branges zeta-zero inverse-spectral program is cited as context for inverse spectral theory, not as a structural classification exception or a disproof.
The zeta-zero inverse-spectral program is cited as a historical target, not as a structurally sporadic object or a refutation.
No actual inverse-spectral operator is specified, only de Branges’ unsuccessful program is mentioned illustratively.

examples.name: Semicircle law from the rooted half-line limit
examples.note: The local weak limit of the rescaled tridiagonal graph at the root gives the half-line, whose rooted spectral-measure moments are Dyck paths, illustrating how matrix laws can be read from operator limits.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker takes the rooted local limit of the tridiagonal graph, identifies the half-line, and checks via moments/Dyck paths that its root spectral measure is semicircular

passes.reason: The rooted half-line limit is the collapsed deterministic local limit after the random weights and diagonals vanish under scaling.
The rooted half-line is the collapsed deterministic local limit with the random labels and loops disappearing.
The semicircle law is an even, highly regular limiting spectral measure derived from the rooted half-line computation.
the rooted unweighted half-line is a deterministic collapsed local limit with maximal regularity, used to derive the semicircle law
The rooted half-line limit is the collapsed deterministic local limit with noise and diagonal terms killed, yielding the semicircle law.
The rooted half-line is a deterministic local limit in which the random tridiagonal structure has collapsed to a minimal constant-coefficient graph.
the deterministic unweighted half-line has a highly regular root spectral measure, the symmetric semicircle law.
The rooted half-line limit is a collapsed deterministic local limit of the random tridiagonal graph, used to derive the semicircle law.
the scaled model collapses to a constant-weight rooted half-line giving the semicircle law
The constant-coefficient rooted half-line is a highly regular deterministic limit used to derive the semicircle law.
the scaled local limit collapses the random Jacobi data to the deterministic regular rooted half-line whose root spectral measure gives the semicircle law
The scaled local graph collapses to a deterministic noiseless half-line whose root spectral measure is semicircular.

examples.name: Quartic potential giving nearest-neighbor Markov dependence
examples.note: Expanding the trace for a degree-four potential visibly produces only distance-one interactions among Jacobi coefficients, leading directly to the finite-memory Markov/Gibbs-chain idea.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker expands the trace term for the quartic case into length-four returning paths and checks that only distance-one variables interact, yielding a Markov dependence structure

passes.reason: A quartic potential producing nearest-neighbor Markov dependence is a standard low-degree polynomial case used illustratively, not a sporadic or failing case.

examples.name: Noiseless Airy-operator comparison for eigenvalue growth
examples.note: Comparison of the stochastic Airy operator with the ordinary Airy operator and its Airy-function zeros demonstrates an easy consequence of the operator description after the main convergence target has been stated.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker compares the stochastic Airy operator with the noiseless Airy operator, identifies noiseless eigenvalues with Airy zeros, and derives the asymptotic eigenvalue growth

passes.reason: The noiseless Airy operator is the deterministic beta-infinity boundary case of the stochastic Airy family used for comparison bounds.

examples.name: Tracy-Widom tail bounds from Rayleigh quotients
examples.note: The speaker sketches how deterministic test functions in the stochastic Airy quadratic form reduce part of the Tracy-Widom tail estimate to a Gaussian tail calculation, illustrating the probabilistic usefulness of the operator.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker explains applying the Rayleigh quotient with deterministic test functions to the stochastic Airy operator to get Gaussian estimates and hence Tracy-Widom tail bounds

passes.reason: The Tracy-Widom law concerns the soft-edge largest-eigenvalue regime, and the Rayleigh quotients are used to prove tail estimates, not to refute a claim.
The Tracy-Widom law is the soft-edge law for the top eigenvalue whose tails are being bounded.
Tracy-Widom tails concern the edge/largest-eigenvalue distribution, an extremal spectral object proved via Rayleigh quotients.
Tracy-Widom tails concern the edge law of the extremal eigenvalue and are estimated rather than used adversarially
Tracy-Widom tails concern the spectral-edge law for the extremal eigenvalue and are bounded by Rayleigh quotient tests.
Rayleigh quotients are intrinsically variational min-max objects used here to derive Tracy-Widom tail bounds.
Tracy-Widom tails concern the edge or largest-eigenvalue law and are derived constructively via Rayleigh quotients.
The Tracy-Widom law concerns the spectral-edge extreme eigenvalue and is used here for tail estimates via Rayleigh quotients.
Tracy-Widom is the edge top-eigenvalue law whose tails are bounded via Rayleigh quotients
Tracy-Widom tail bounds concern the edge/top-eigenvalue distribution and are used as a positive Rayleigh-quotient application.
Tracy-Widom tails concern the edge law for the top eigenvalue, an extremal spectral statistic, derived via Rayleigh quotient bounds

examples.name: High Airy points converging to the sine process
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker notes that suitably shifted high-index Airy-beta points become bulk-like and converge to the sine-kernel process.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker states that shifted high Airy points converge to the sine-kernel process as a corollary, without working through the convergence

passes.reason: High-index Airy points come from the soft-edge Airy spectrum and are used to show a bulk sine-process limit rather than a failure.
